title: Hypopituitarism
oneLiner: Condition of partial or complete deficiency of one or more pituitary hormones; can be life-threatening due to secondary adrenal insufficiency or pituitary apoplexy.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Pituitary apoplexy:** Sudden, severe headache + visual loss/ophthalmoplegia + hypotension/ altered GCS → urgent CT/MRI brain + high-dose steroids + neurosurgery referral.
- **Acute secondary adrenal crisis:** Shock, hyponatraemia, hypoglycaemia in a known pituitary patient or with undiagnosed hypopituitarism (e.g. after surgery, infection). Single finding: **hypotension refractory to fluids + low random cortisol** → give hydrocortisone 100 mg IV immediately before any other tests.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - **Adrenal insufficiency (ACTH deficiency):** fatigue, weight loss, postural dizziness, pallor (no hyperpigmentation as in primary), hyponatraemia, hypoglycaemia.
  - **Thyroid deficiency (TSH deficiency):** cold intolerance, constipation, bradycardia, dry skin, hoarse voice.
  - **Gonadal deficiency (LH/FSH):** amenorrhoea/oligomenorrhoea, loss of libido, erectile dysfunction, loss of axillary/pubic hair.
  - **GH deficiency:** decreased muscle mass, central adiposity, fatigue (often subtle).
  - **Prolactin deficiency:** failure to lactate postpartum.
  - **Antidiuretic hormone (ADH) deficiency (rare in global hypopituitarism):** central diabetes insipidus (polyuria, polydipsia).
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Postural BP drop (>20 systolic), cool peripheries, bradycardia.
  - Visual field defects (bitemporal hemianopia) if pituitary mass present.
  - Pale, fine skin; absence of axillary hair.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - Primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison’s) – hyperpigmentation, hyponatraemia + hyperkalaemia (vs secondary: hyponatraemia alone, normal K+).
  - Primary hypothyroidism – TSH high; secondary hypothyroidism: TSH low/normal with low FT4.
  - Sheehan’s syndrome (postpartum haemorrhage) – failure of lactation + persistent amenorrhoea.
  - Eating disorders, chronic illness – can mimic GH/thyroid deficiency but normal pituitary hormones.
  - Panhypopituitarism due to pituitary tumour/surgery/radiation.
- **Splitter:** No hyperpigmentation + hyponatraemia without hyperkalaemia → secondary adrenal insufficiency (Addison’s usually has both hyperkalaemia and pigmentation).
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Urgent blood glucose (capillary) – hypoglycaemia common.
  - Urine output + urine osmolality if polyuria suspected (central DI).
  - ECG if bradycardia or hypothermia.
- **Bloods:**
  - Morning cortisol (0900) – <100 nmol/L suggests deficiency; >500 nmol/L excludes.
  - ACTH, TSH, FT4, LH, FSH, prolactin, IGF-1, free testosterone (men), oestradiol (women).
  - U&E, glucose, calcium, phosphate, LFT, coagulation.
  - If acute: random cortisol (any time, but interpret with caution) + ACTH.
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** MRI pituitary with contrast (structural lesion, apoplexy).
  - **Second-line:** CT head if MRI unavailable or contraindicated (apoplexy often visible).
- **Special tests:**
  - Short synacthen test (250 µg IM/IV) – cortisol <430 nmol/L at 30/60 min indicates adrenal insufficiency (but may be normal in recent secondary deficiency).
  - Insulin tolerance test (gold standard for GH and ACTH axis) – only in specialist unit.
  - Water deprivation test if DI suspected.
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - No single unified score; diagnosis based on low baseline morning cortisol (<100 nmol/L) plus low/normal ACTH (secondary) or low FT4 + low/normal TSH (secondary hypothyroidism).
- **Never-miss test:** **Random serum cortisol** (or morning cortisol) in any patient with unexplained hypotension, hyponatraemia, or hypoglycaemia – missing this delays life-saving steroids.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **If adrenal crisis suspected:** Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV stat, then 50 mg IV q6h. No waiting for test results.
  - IV 0.9% saline 500 mL bolus over 15 min, repeat as needed.
  - Treat hypoglycaemia with 50 mL 50% dextrose IV.
  - **If apoplexy suspected:** Immediate neurosurgical referral + high-dose steroids (hydrocortisone 100 mg IV) + urgent MRI.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue stress-dose steroids for 24-72h, then taper to oral (hydrocortisone 10-20 mg morning, 5-10 mg afternoon).
  - Start levothyroxine **only after** adequate cortisol replacement – 25-50 µg PO daily initially, increase slowly (target FT4 mid-normal).
  - Hormone replacement: testosterone (men) / oestrogen + progesterone (women) – refer to endocrinology.
  - Fluid balance monitoring, daily U&E, glucose.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Lifelong hormone replacement, titrated by clinical response and hormone levels.
  - Stress dosing (double/triple oral dose or IM/IV) during illness/surgery.
  - Annual MRI if pituitary tumour residual.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: 0.9% saline at maintenance (usually 1 L/8h) – avoid hypotonic fluids.
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) if no contraindication.
  - Diet: NBM if urgent imaging/ surgery; otherwise free.
  - Observations: NEWS2 q4h (q1h if unstable).
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line:**
  - **Hydrocortisone:** 10–20 mg PO morning, 5–10 mg PO afternoon (usual adult). Stress doses: 50–100 mg IV q6h.
  - **Levothyroxine:** 25–50 µg PO daily initially (starting dose 25 µg if >60 years or cardiac disease). Titrate by FT4.
  - **Testosterone:** gel 50 mg once daily (apply to clean dry skin) or IM injections (e.g. Sustanon 250 mg every 3 weeks) – specialist initiation.
  - **Oestradiol transdermal:** 25–50 µg patch twice weekly + progesterone (if uterus present).
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Prednisolone (3–4 mg PO daily) – less circadian; not preferred.
  - GH (somatropin) – specialist only, contraindicated in active malignancy.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Hydrocortisone with live vaccines (avoid).
  - Levothyroxine with iron, calcium, antacids (separate by 4 h).
  - Testosterone with anticoagulants (monitor INR).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No specific dose changes for thyroid/adrenal hormones; but monitor fluid balance in renal impairment (steroids cause fluid retention).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Starting levothyroxine before ensuring adequate cortisol replacement** (can precipitate adrenal crisis). Always check cortisol status or start steroids first.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Adrenal crisis (hypotension, hyponatraemia, hypoglycaemia, coma).
  - Pituitary apoplexy (visual loss, stroke-like symptoms, herniation).
  - Secondary infection due to immunosuppression (if high-dose steroids).
- **Chronic:**
  - Osteoporosis (sex hormone deficiency).
  - Infertility (gonadotropin deficiency).
  - Increased cardiovascular risk (GH/thyroid deficiency).
  - Empty sella syndrome (after apoplexy or surgery).
sections.Prognosis: - Excellent with appropriate hormone replacement (normal life expectancy). Apoplexy carries risk of permanent vision loss or death if untreated. Lifelong follow-up required.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **In any unexplained hyponatraemia + hypotension, give IV hydrocortisone first, then check cortisol** – the result will be low anyway, and you’ve prevented a crisis.
- **Check random cortisol immediately in patients with known pituitary disease and acute illness** – the dose may need doubling.
- **Document a “steroid card” and MedicAlert bracelet** for all new hypopituitarism diagnoses.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not start levothyroxine before steroids** – this can trigger adrenal crisis (thyroid hormone increases cortisol metabolism).
- **Do not rely on a single normal random cortisol to rule out secondary adrenal insufficiency** – if strong clinical suspicion (e.g. hypotension, known pituitary disease), give steroids.
- **Do not forget to double oral steroids for minor illness (fever, vomiting, surgery)** – this is the most common precipitant of adrenal crisis.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Inability to take oral steroids (vomiting, severe illness), severe headache, visual changes, confusion, or syncope.
- **Follow-up:** Endocrinology clinic within 2–4 weeks; baseline MRI follow-up if indicated.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / endocrinology reg if:** NEWS2 ≥5, lactate >2 mmol/L, GCS <15, refractory hypotension after two fluid boluses, new focal neurology or severe headache (suggest apoplexy – also call neurosurgery).
- **Crash team:** Cardiac arrest or GCS <9.

rawMarkdown:
## Hypopituitarism
**One-liner:** Condition of partial or complete deficiency of one or more pituitary hormones; can be life-threatening due to secondary adrenal insufficiency or pituitary apoplexy.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Pituitary apoplexy:** Sudden, severe headache + visual loss/ophthalmoplegia + hypotension/ altered GCS → urgent CT/MRI brain + high-dose steroids + neurosurgery referral.
- **Acute secondary adrenal crisis:** Shock, hyponatraemia, hypoglycaemia in a known pituitary patient or with undiagnosed hypopituitarism (e.g. after surgery, infection). Single finding: **hypotension refractory to fluids + low random cortisol** → give hydrocortisone 100 mg IV immediately before any other tests.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - **Adrenal insufficiency (ACTH deficiency):** fatigue, weight loss, postural dizziness, pallor (no hyperpigmentation as in primary), hyponatraemia, hypoglycaemia.
  - **Thyroid deficiency (TSH deficiency):** cold intolerance, constipation, bradycardia, dry skin, hoarse voice.
  - **Gonadal deficiency (LH/FSH):** amenorrhoea/oligomenorrhoea, loss of libido, erectile dysfunction, loss of axillary/pubic hair.
  - **GH deficiency:** decreased muscle mass, central adiposity, fatigue (often subtle).
  - **Prolactin deficiency:** failure to lactate postpartum.
  - **Antidiuretic hormone (ADH) deficiency (rare in global hypopituitarism):** central diabetes insipidus (polyuria, polydipsia).
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Postural BP drop (>20 systolic), cool peripheries, bradycardia.
  - Visual field defects (bitemporal hemianopia) if pituitary mass present.
  - Pale, fine skin; absence of axillary hair.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - Primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison’s) – hyperpigmentation, hyponatraemia + hyperkalaemia (vs secondary: hyponatraemia alone, normal K+).
  - Primary hypothyroidism – TSH high; secondary hypothyroidism: TSH low/normal with low FT4.
  - Sheehan’s syndrome (postpartum haemorrhage) – failure of lactation + persistent amenorrhoea.
  - Eating disorders, chronic illness – can mimic GH/thyroid deficiency but normal pituitary hormones.
  - Panhypopituitarism due to pituitary tumour/surgery/radiation.
- **Splitter:** No hyperpigmentation + hyponatraemia without hyperkalaemia → secondary adrenal insufficiency (Addison’s usually has both hyperkalaemia and pigmentation).

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Urgent blood glucose (capillary) – hypoglycaemia common.
  - Urine output + urine osmolality if polyuria suspected (central DI).
  - ECG if bradycardia or hypothermia.
- **Bloods:**
  - Morning cortisol (0900) – <100 nmol/L suggests deficiency; >500 nmol/L excludes.
  - ACTH, TSH, FT4, LH, FSH, prolactin, IGF-1, free testosterone (men), oestradiol (women).
  - U&E, glucose, calcium, phosphate, LFT, coagulation.
  - If acute: random cortisol (any time, but interpret with caution) + ACTH.
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** MRI pituitary with contrast (structural lesion, apoplexy).
  - **Second-line:** CT head if MRI unavailable or contraindicated (apoplexy often visible).
- **Special tests:**
  - Short synacthen test (250 µg IM/IV) – cortisol <430 nmol/L at 30/60 min indicates adrenal insufficiency (but may be normal in recent secondary deficiency).
  - Insulin tolerance test (gold standard for GH and ACTH axis) – only in specialist unit.
  - Water deprivation test if DI suspected.
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - No single unified score; diagnosis based on low baseline morning cortisol (<100 nmol/L) plus low/normal ACTH (secondary) or low FT4 + low/normal TSH (secondary hypothyroidism).
- **Never-miss test:** **Random serum cortisol** (or morning cortisol) in any patient with unexplained hypotension, hyponatraemia, or hypoglycaemia – missing this delays life-saving steroids.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **If adrenal crisis suspected:** Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV stat, then 50 mg IV q6h. No waiting for test results.
  - IV 0.9% saline 500 mL bolus over 15 min, repeat as needed.
  - Treat hypoglycaemia with 50 mL 50% dextrose IV.
  - **If apoplexy suspected:** Immediate neurosurgical referral + high-dose steroids (hydrocortisone 100 mg IV) + urgent MRI.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue stress-dose steroids for 24-72h, then taper to oral (hydrocortisone 10-20 mg morning, 5-10 mg afternoon).
  - Start levothyroxine **only after** adequate cortisol replacement – 25-50 µg PO daily initially, increase slowly (target FT4 mid-normal).
  - Hormone replacement: testosterone (men) / oestrogen + progesterone (women) – refer to endocrinology.
  - Fluid balance monitoring, daily U&E, glucose.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Lifelong hormone replacement, titrated by clinical response and hormone levels.
  - Stress dosing (double/triple oral dose or IM/IV) during illness/surgery.
  - Annual MRI if pituitary tumour residual.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: 0.9% saline at maintenance (usually 1 L/8h) – avoid hypotonic fluids.
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) if no contraindication.
  - Diet: NBM if urgent imaging/ surgery; otherwise free.
  - Observations: NEWS2 q4h (q1h if unstable).

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line:**
  - **Hydrocortisone:** 10–20 mg PO morning, 5–10 mg PO afternoon (usual adult). Stress doses: 50–100 mg IV q6h.
  - **Levothyroxine:** 25–50 µg PO daily initially (starting dose 25 µg if >60 years or cardiac disease). Titrate by FT4.
  - **Testosterone:** gel 50 mg once daily (apply to clean dry skin) or IM injections (e.g. Sustanon 250 mg every 3 weeks) – specialist initiation.
  - **Oestradiol transdermal:** 25–50 µg patch twice weekly + progesterone (if uterus present).
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Prednisolone (3–4 mg PO daily) – less circadian; not preferred.
  - GH (somatropin) – specialist only, contraindicated in active malignancy.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Hydrocortisone with live vaccines (avoid).
  - Levothyroxine with iron, calcium, antacids (separate by 4 h).
  - Testosterone with anticoagulants (monitor INR).
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No specific dose changes for thyroid/adrenal hormones; but monitor fluid balance in renal impairment (steroids cause fluid retention).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Starting levothyroxine before ensuring adequate cortisol replacement** (can precipitate adrenal crisis). Always check cortisol status or start steroids first.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Adrenal crisis (hypotension, hyponatraemia, hypoglycaemia, coma).
  - Pituitary apoplexy (visual loss, stroke-like symptoms, herniation).
  - Secondary infection due to immunosuppression (if high-dose steroids).
- **Chronic:**
  - Osteoporosis (sex hormone deficiency).
  - Infertility (gonadotropin deficiency).
  - Increased cardiovascular risk (GH/thyroid deficiency).
  - Empty sella syndrome (after apoplexy or surgery).

### Prognosis
- Excellent with appropriate hormone replacement (normal life expectancy). Apoplexy carries risk of permanent vision loss or death if untreated. Lifelong follow-up required.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **In any unexplained hyponatraemia + hypotension, give IV hydrocortisone first, then check cortisol** – the result will be low anyway, and you’ve prevented a crisis.
- **Check random cortisol immediately in patients with known pituitary disease and acute illness** – the dose may need doubling.
- **Document a “steroid card” and MedicAlert bracelet** for all new hypopituitarism diagnoses.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not start levothyroxine before steroids** – this can trigger adrenal crisis (thyroid hormone increases cortisol metabolism).
- **Do not rely on a single normal random cortisol to rule out secondary adrenal insufficiency** – if strong clinical suspicion (e.g. hypotension, known pituitary disease), give steroids.
- **Do not forget to double oral steroids for minor illness (fever, vomiting, surgery)** – this is the most common precipitant of adrenal crisis.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Inability to take oral steroids (vomiting, severe illness), severe headache, visual changes, confusion, or syncope.
- **Follow-up:** Endocrinology clinic within 2–4 weeks; baseline MRI follow-up if indicated.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / endocrinology reg if:** NEWS2 ≥5, lactate >2 mmol/L, GCS <15, refractory hypotension after two fluid boluses, new focal neurology or severe headache (suggest apoplexy – also call neurosurgery).
- **Crash team:** Cardiac arrest or GCS <9.
